coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots/Cu2MoS4 nanosheets hybrids with efficient photoelectrochemical performance
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Ning
Wei, Bo
Cao, Wanlu
Gao, Hong
Xu, Lingling
coredata.dc:description: We report a facile fabrication of carbon dots (CDs)/Cu2MoS4 nanosheets hybrid photocathodes for potential application in photoelectrochemical cells (PECs). The structural, morphological and photoelectrochemical characteristics are studied. Compared with pristine Cu2MoS4, the photoelectrocatalytic activity of CDs/Cu2MoS4 nanosheets composites was significantly improved under artificial solar light, as confirmed by the lowered onset potential and improved photocurrent response. Our study provides useful insights in the design of low-cost and earth abundant materials for high-efficiency solar fuel production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy storage and conversion
Transition metal chalcogenide
Nanocomposites
Photoelectrochemical cell
Photocathode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of Cu2MoS4 and CDs/Cu2MoS4 samples.
(a) SEM, (b) HRTEM and (c) SAED images of Cu2MoS4 viewed down the c axis, (d) SEM, (e) TEM and (f) HRTEM images of CDs/Cu2MoS4.

(a) Photoelectrochemical properties of Cu2MoS4 and CDs/Cu2MoS4 photocathodes under artificial solar light irradiation. (b) Photocurrent density using Cu2MoS4 and CDs/Cu2MoS4 photocathodes without bias. (c) Nyquist plots of Cu2MoS4 and CDs/Cu2MoS4 samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots/Cu2MoS4 nanosheets hybrids with efficient photoelectrochemical performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photonic and Electric Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin 150025, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photonic and Electric Bandgap Materials
School of Physics and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150080, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a facile fabrication of carbon dots (CDs)/Cu2MoS4 nanosheets hybrid photocathodes for potential application in photoelectrochemical cells (PECs). The structural, morphological and photoelectrochemical characteristics are studied. Compared with pristine Cu2MoS4, the photoelectrocatalytic activity of CDs/Cu2MoS4 nanosheets composites was significantly improved under artificial solar light, as confirmed by the lowered onset potential and improved photocurrent response. Our study provides useful insights in the design of low-cost and earth abundant materials for high-efficiency solar fuel production.

ce:section.ce:para: Water splitting using semiconductor-based photoelectrochemical cells (PECs) has received considerable attention as a promising approach for solar energy harvesting and storage [1–3]. The performance of PEC devices is fundamentally related to the rational design of photoelectrodes and tailored nanostructures [4]. In the past years, layered transition metal chalcogenides (TMCs) like MoS2 and WS2 have been widely investigated as promising photoelectrodes because of their excellent absorption ability on visible-light region and extensive source [4,5]. Recently, some reports had been devoted to ternary transition metal chalcogenides like Cu2WS4, Cu2MoS4, Cu2MoSe4 and Cu2WSe4 [6–12]. As a typical ternary transition metal chalcogenides, Cu2MoS4 (bandgap: 1.71eV) is considered to be a commendable semiconductor material because its earth-abundant, environment friendly and low-cost [8–11]. Liang et al. reported that sheet-like Cu2MoS4 exhibits good electrocatalytic activity for H2 generation under visible-light irradiation [9]. Recently, Yang et al. found that nanosized Cu2MoS4 co-catalyst can promote H2 photogeneration on a Cu2O photocathode with a significant anodic shift of onset potential [10].

Herein, we reported that the photocathode performance of Cu2MoS4 nanosheets can be improved by the deposition of carbon dots (CDs). The lower onset potential and the higher photocurrent density of CDs/Cu2MoS4 sample demonstrated more efficient charge separation and transportation in the CDs/Cu2MoS4 sample in comparison with that of pure Cu2MoS4.

Cu2MoS4 nanosheets were prepared using Cu2O as template [13]. Typically, 30mg of Na2MoO4·2H2O and 54mg of C2H5NS were added in 20ml ethylene glycol. Then, 20mg of Cu2O powder was added and the precursor was transferred into a 45ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave that was maintained at 200°C for 24h. The final product was thoroughly washed and dried. For carbon dots preparation, 0.75g of sucrose was added in 30ml ultrapure water with magnetic stirring. Then, the solution was transferred into a 45ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 5h. The final product was obtained by centrifugation the suspension [15]. The formation mechanism of carbon dots is based on the carbonization of sucrose, which was briefly illustrated in Fig. S1. Cu2MoS4 based electrodes were prepared by a drop-casting method on fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) substrates (1×1cm2). The carbon dots (0.2–0.6mg/mL carbon solutions) were then deposited on the surface of the Cu2MoS4 electrode, followed by drying at 60°C in vacuum for 1h (CDs/Cu2MoS4).

A Rigaku D/max-2600/PC X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Cu-Kα radiation) was used to determine the composition and structure. A FEI Tecai G2 F20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM, 200kV) and a Hitachi SU-70 scanning electron microscope (SEM) were employed to observe sample morphology. Raman spectra were collected by using a LabRAM HR800 Raman spectroscopy with a 488nm laser as excitation source. The specific surface areas were determined according to the Brunauer−Emmett−Teller (BET) method using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020. The photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of the photocathodes were measured by a three-electrode setup in 1.0M H2SO4 electrolyte (pH≈0.5). The Cu2MoS4 based films, a Pt plate, and an Ag/AgCl (KCl saturated) electrode were used as the working, counter and reference electrodes, respectively. A 300W Xe lamp was used as the light source to simulate the sunlight irradiation. Linear-sweep voltammograms (LSV) and the transient photocurrent measurements were carried out by an electrochemical workstation (Chenhua CHI-660E).

Fig.1 a shows the XRD patterns of Cu2MoS4 and CDs/Cu2MoS4 samples. The diffraction peaks in Fig.1a can be well matched to I-phase Cu2MoS4 with no detection of additional peaks, indicating that phase-pure Cu2MoS4 was successfully synthesized [13]. After the deposition of carbon dots, a new weak peak at ∼25.5° was observed, which can be assigned to the (002) peak of carbon materials [14]. But this peak is very weak because of the small quantity of carbon deposition. Typical Raman spectra of two samples are shown in Fig.1b. For the pristine Cu2MoS4, three strong Raman peaks centered at 433cm−1, 866cm−1 and 1299cm−1 belong to E mode, while the rest peaks located at 636cm−1, 217cm−1 and 118cm−1 belongs to A1 mode, B1 mode and B2 mode, respectively, consistent with previous report [15]. As for CDs/Cu2MoS4 sample, it is noteworthy that two additional broad characteristic peaks at about 1360cm−1 and 1590cm−1 can be clearly observed, corresponding to the D-band and the G-band of carbon materials, respectively [15]. The D-band is associated with the in-phase vibration of the graphite lattice, while the G-band is attributed to the ordered graphite structure. Both XRD and Raman profiles suggest CDs/Cu2MoS4 composite was successfully prepared.

Fig.2 a shows the SEM image of Cu2MoS4 with a general characteristic of nanosheet morphology. The side length is about 1–2μm and the thickness is about 30–60nm. A high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, Fig.2b) and the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED, Fig.2c) pattern confirm the Cu2MoS4 nanosheets were single crystals with high crystallinity. The lattice spacing of 0.51nm corresponds to the (002) plane of I-phase Cu2MoS4 [13]. The SEM image of CDs/Cu2MoS4 hybrid in Fig.2d reveals the rough surface of nanosheets due to the deposition of many carbon dots (average size: ∼25nm). TEM and HRTEM images in Fig.2e-f show good bonding between two phases probably bonded by the van der Waals force. The lattice spacing of 0.32nm corresponds to the (002) plane of carbon [14], further confirming the formation of CDs/Cu2MoS4 composite.

The photoelectrochemical properties of the two samples were compared. The LSV profiles of Cu2MoS4 and CDs/Cu2MoS4 films are shown in Fig.3 a. The potential was swept linearly at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 between −0.5 and 0.0V vs. Ag/AgCl in 1.0M H2SO4 electrolyte. Notably, the photocurrent density of Cu2MoS4 is almost zero between −0.2 and 0.0V vs. Ag/AgCl electrode under the illumination. The photocurrent density of CDs/Cu2MoS4 photocathode demonstrates a rapid growth from −0.25VAg/AgCl to the cathodic direction. Furthermore, the onset potential of the photocurrent generation shifts from −0.363VAg/AgCl for Cu2MoS4 to −0.349VAg/AgCl for CDs/Cu2MoS4. Under the simulated solar light illumination and zero applied bias versus Ag/AgCl, the CDs/Cu2MoS4 electrode shows a greater photocurrent density than that of the Cu2MoS4 electrode (Fig.3b). To study the role of carbon concentration, several composite samples using 0.2–0.6mgmL−1 carbon solutions were prepared and compared. The LSV profiles in Fig. S2. clearly reveals that the CDs/Cu2MoS4 composite with 0.4mgmL−1 concentration exhibits the best performance (also much higher than that of standard Cu2O electrode). The lowered onset potential and the increased photocurrent density demonstrate more efficient charge separation and transportation in the CDs/Cu2MoS4 films, compared with the pure Cu2MoS4 film. Fig.3c shows the Nyquist plots of Cu2MoS4 and CDs/Cu2MoS4 photocathodes, consisting of two semiarcs in the high-frequency and low-frequency ranges. The high frequency arcs can be assigned to the electron transport between electrode particles and particles/FTO substrate at the electrodes, while the low-frequency loops may be attributed to the large resistance at the electrode-electrolyte interface [16]. In the high-frequency range, the diameter of the semiarc for the CDs/Cu2MoS4 photoelectrode is much smaller than that of the Cu2MoS4, which indicates that its charge separation efficiency is superior to that of pristine Cu2MoS4. Therefore, the CDs/Cu2MoS4 photoelectrode exhibits the better photoelectrochemical performance.

In the CDs/Cu2MoS4 PEC system, owing to the high conductivity of carbon nanocrystallines, electrons could be shuttled rapidly through the interface between carbon nanocrystallines and Cu2MoS4, and therefore the chance of charge recombination is dramatically decreased [17]. Carbon dots can absorb more energy from the full spectrum, broadening the scope of CDs/Cu2MoS4 absorption [15]. Moreover, the specific area of CDs/Cu2MoS4 (∼17.5m2 g−1) is higher than that of unmodified Cu2MoS4 (∼12.6m2 g−1). The composites with more coarsen surface and larger electrochemically activesurfacearea can facilitate the transport of reactants to the active sites, making the photoelectrochemical process more efficient. Consequently, the PEC hydrogen generation performance of CDs/Cu2MoS4 was improved significantly compared with pristine Cu2MoS4.

In summary, the CDs/Cu2MoS4 hybrids were synthesized via a facile and low-cost method. The combined XRD, Raman and microstructure results confirmed that CDs/Cu2MoS4 composites were successfully prepared with good interface. The PEC test demonstrated the photoelectrocatalytic properties of CDs/Cu2MoS4 were significantly improved compared with fresh Cu2MoS4. Our study shows that coupling the carbon nanodots is an effective way to enhance the photoelectrochemical activity of photoelectrodes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science Foundation of Heilongjiang province (A2015005), Innovative Talents Fund of Harbin (2014RFQXJ085) and Open Project from Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials (PEBM 201209).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2017.03.101.
